subtopic: Application of Junction Diode as a Rectifier
title: How diodes rectify AC into DC
summary: Because a diode conducts only one way, it converts AC to DC: a half-wave rectifier uses one half-cycle (one diode); a full-wave or bridge rectifier uses both half-cycles.
body:
**Must know**
- Half-wave (1 diode): conducts on alternate half-cycles; ripple frequency $f_{out}=f$; average output $V_{dc}=\dfrac{V_m}{\pi}$.
- Full-wave / bridge (2 / 4 diodes): both half-cycles used; ripple frequency $f_{out}=2f$; average output $V_{dc}=\dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}$.
- A capacitor filter smooths the pulsating output toward steady DC.
**Exam traps**
- Full-wave ripple frequency is $2f$, half-wave is $f$; a bridge rectifier uses four diodes.
- A rectifier converts AC → DC (not the reverse).

subtopic: Classification of Metals, Conductors and Semiconductors
title: Why the energy band gap separates conductors, semiconductors and insulators
summary: Solids are classified by the band gap E_g between the valence and conduction bands: metals have overlapping bands (E_g ≈ 0), semiconductors a small gap (~1 eV), and insulators a large gap (> 3 eV).
body:
**Must know**
- Conductors (metals): conduction and valence bands overlap, $E_g\approx0$, so many free electrons.
- Insulators: a large gap, $E_g>3\ \text{eV}$ (diamond $\approx6\ \text{eV}$).
- Semiconductors: a small gap, $E_g\approx1\ \text{eV}$ (Si $\approx1.1\ \text{eV}$, Ge $\approx0.7\ \text{eV}$).
**Exam traps**
- A semiconductor's resistance DECREASES as temperature rises (negative temperature coefficient) — the opposite of a metal.
- At absolute zero a pure semiconductor behaves as an insulator.

formulas.meaning: Band gaps of silicon and germanium

title: How doping creates n-type and p-type semiconductors
summary: Doping with pentavalent donors gives an n-type (electron-majority) semiconductor; trivalent acceptors give a p-type (hole-majority) one — both greatly increasing conductivity while staying electrically neutral.
body:
**Must know**
- n-type: dope with pentavalent atoms (P, As, Sb) → extra electrons; majority carriers are electrons ($n_e\gg n_h$).
- p-type: dope with trivalent atoms (B, Al, In) → holes; majority carriers are holes ($n_h\gg n_e$).
- The mass-action law still holds: $n_e n_h=n_i^2$.
**Exam traps**
- A doped semiconductor is overall electrically neutral, despite having majority carriers.
- Donors (pentavalent) → n-type; acceptors (trivalent) → p-type.

title: How a pure semiconductor conducts through electron–hole pairs
summary: In a pure (intrinsic) semiconductor, thermal energy breaks covalent bonds to create equal numbers of free electrons and holes, so n_e = n_h = n_i.
body:
**Must know**
- Equal carriers: $n_e=n_h=n_i$, the intrinsic carrier concentration.
- Conduction is by free electrons (in the conduction band) and holes (in the valence band).
- $n_i$ increases with temperature, so conductivity rises with $T$.
**Exam traps**
- Electrons and holes are present in equal numbers in an intrinsic semiconductor.
- At $T=0$ K it acts as an insulator (no broken bonds).

title: Objective I
summary: An **LED** is a p-n junction operated in **forward bias**.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- In a semiconductor, charge carriers (electrons and holes) are generated by thermal excitation across the band gap. More thermal energy → more carriers → higher conductivity. This is opposite to metals (where conductivity decreases due to increased phonon scattering). Mathematically, carrier density $\propto e^{-E_g/(2k_BT)}$, so conductivity increases exponentially with temperature. Answer: (A).
- At 0 K, no thermal energy is available to excite electrons across the band gap. The valence band is completely filled and the conduction band is completely empty. No charge carriers exist → the semiconductor behaves as a **perfect insulator** at 0 K. As temperature rises, carriers are generated and conductivity increases. Answer: (B).
- **n-type semiconductor**: formed by doping with pentavalent impurity atoms (e.g., phosphorus in silicon). Each dopant contributes an extra electron to the conduction band. The majority carriers are **electrons** (n for negative). The minority carriers are holes (thermally generated). The material remains electrically neutral overall. Answer: (B).
- **Forward bias**: p-side at higher potential (positive terminal to p-side). The external field reduces the built-in potential barrier at the junction, allowing majority carriers to diffuse across: holes from p to n, electrons from n to p. The depletion region narrows → low resistance → **large current** flows. Reverse bias: depletion region widens → high resistance → very small (leakage) current. Answer: (B).
- A **Zener diode** is designed to operate in reverse breakdown (Zener/avalanche breakdown) at a specific **Zener voltage** $V_Z$. Once in breakdown, the voltage across it remains essentially constant regardless of current. This property makes it ideal as a **voltage regulator** — it maintains a stable output voltage even when input voltage or load resistance varies. Answer: (C).
- In a common-emitter (CE) configuration: $$\beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} \quad (\text{DC current gain})$$ $\beta$ is typically 50–500. The small base current $I_B$ controls the larger collector current $I_C$, providing **current amplification**. Also: $I_E = I_B + I_C$; $\alpha = I_C/I_E = \beta/(\beta+1)$. Answer: (B).
- An **LED** is a p-n junction operated in **forward bias**. Electrons injected from the n-side and holes from the p-side recombine in the junction region. In direct-bandgap semiconductors (GaAs, GaP, etc.), this recombination releases a photon with energy $\approx E_g$ (the band gap). The wavelength of emitted light: $\lambda = hc/E_g$, which can be designed to be in the visible, infrared, or UV range. Answer: (B).

title: Objective II
summary: - **A** ✓: Si (Group 14) and Ge (Group 14) are classic elemental semiconductors.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- - **A** ✓: Forward bias reduces the built-in potential → depletion region narrows. - **B** ✓: Reverse bias reinforces the built-in potential → depletion region widens. - **C** ✗: A diode conducts significantly only in **forward bias** (rectifying action). In reverse bias, only a tiny leakage current flows (until breakdown). - **D** ✓: Silicon diode threshold voltage $\approx 0.7\,\rm V$ (germanium $\approx 0.3\,\rm V$). Answer: (A), (B), (D).
- - **A** ✓: Si (Group 14) and Ge (Group 14) are classic elemental semiconductors. - **B** ✗: Pentavalent impurities (5 valence electrons, e.g., P, As, Sb) donate electrons → **n-type**. Trivalent impurities create **p-type**. - **C** ✓: In an intrinsic semiconductor, thermal generation always creates equal numbers of electrons and holes (electron-hole pairs). - **D** ✓: Conductors: $E_g = 0$ (overlapping bands); Insulators: $E_g > 5\,\rm eV$; Semiconductors: $E_g \sim 1\,\rm eV$ (Si: 1.1 eV, Ge: 0.67 eV). Answer: (A), (C), (D).

title: How semiconductor devices build up from bands and junctions
summary: Semiconductor electronics builds from band theory → intrinsic/extrinsic semiconductors → the p-n junction → the diode and its rectifier applications.
body:
**Must know**
- The band gap classifies solids; doping tunes conductivity into n-type or p-type.
- A p-n junction forms a depletion region and barrier → the diode conducts one way → rectifiers convert AC to DC.
- Silicon ($E_g\approx1.1\ \text{eV}$) and germanium ($\approx0.7\ \text{eV}$) are the workhorse materials.
**Exam traps**
- Semiconductors have a negative temperature coefficient of resistance.
- A diode conducts in forward bias; a rectifier converts AC → DC.

title: Why a diode conducts only under forward bias
summary: A p-n junction diode conducts easily in forward bias (barrier lowered) but blocks current in reverse bias (only a tiny saturation current flows) — it is a one-way valve for current.
body:
**Must know**
- Forward bias (p to +): barrier and depletion width shrink; current rises steeply past the knee voltage ($\approx0.7\ \text{V}$ Si, $0.3\ \text{V}$ Ge).
- Reverse bias: barrier and width grow; only a small, nearly constant reverse saturation current flows until breakdown.
- Diode (Shockley) equation: $I=I_0\!\left(e^{eV/kT}-1\right)$.
**Exam traps**
- Forward bias = low resistance, reverse bias = high resistance.
- The reverse saturation current is tiny and almost independent of the reverse voltage (until breakdown).

title: How a p-n junction builds a depletion region and a barrier potential
summary: At a p-n junction, carriers diffuse across and leave behind immobile charged ions, forming a depletion region whose built-in potential barrier opposes further diffusion.
body:
**Must know**
- Electrons diffuse n→p and holes p→n, leaving immobile ionised donors/acceptors → a carrier-free depletion region.
- The exposed ions set up a built-in barrier potential ($\approx0.7\ \text{V}$ for Si, $\approx0.3\ \text{V}$ for Ge) that opposes further diffusion.
- Equilibrium is reached when the diffusion current is balanced by the drift current.
**Exam traps**
- The depletion region has essentially no free charge carriers.
- The barrier forms with no external battery connected.

formulas.meaning: Electric field across a depletion width d

title: reNEET2026
summary: Forward bias narrows the depletion region while reverse bias widens it.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- The Zener clamps at 3 V; the remaining $5-3=2$ V appears across the series resistor between B and A.
- Forward bias narrows the depletion region while reverse bias widens it. For the given connection the ordering of depletion widths is $W_3>W_2>W_1.$
